artifacts.content:
Hi Em,

Finally sitting down after getting Lily to bed and half-heartedly loading the dishwasher. I wanted to actually write instead of just texting fragments.

Work has been extra rough. On November 3rd I had what I’m pretty sure was a full-on panic attack in the middle of a status review with Claire at the Northline Beaverton office. It was the one where we were supposed to walk through that $180,000 client renewal plan with the VP of Product and two senior engineers. My heart started pounding, hands shaking so badly I couldn’t even hold my pen, and I ended up locking myself in the 5th-floor women’s restroom for almost 25 minutes while they were literally waiting in the conference room. Since then I keep thinking I’m one mistake away from a formal PIP or just being let go.

It didn’t come out of nowhere either. Ever since Daniel put me on that “informal” performance improvement plan back in March over the missed Aurora sprint deadlines, I’ve been waking up at 3–4 a.m. replaying his “slipping reliability” comment from that one-on-one in Conference Room B. I’ve been blocking off 6–8 a.m. on my calendar for “deep work” and even staying late at the Beaverton office three nights a week, but the more I try to overcorrect, the worse the anxiety gets.

Meanwhile life at home is the usual circus: Lily has a reading log due Friday, Jason’s buried in some launch, and Bailey ate half a sock. I’m trying to still do book club next week, but honestly, I mostly want to hide under a blanket.

Anyway, I just needed to say all that to someone who won’t immediately jump to “have you tried yoga?”

Love you,
M

Mom,

I know we talked on the phone, but I wanted to write this out because money stuff makes my brain spin.

When you fell at your apartment in Salem back in August and ended up at Salem Hospital for the diabetes complications, I put $3,200 on the CareCredit card for the rehab and supplies so they wouldn’t hound you. On top of that, Jason and I were already sitting on about $9,000 in regular credit card debt. No surprise, I’m now waking up at 3 a.m. most nights, logging into our Bank of America account, and obsessively reworking our spreadsheet instead of sleeping.

It’s layered on top of everything else with your health. I keep thinking about that July 18 fall in the Salem General parking lot when Dr. Patel told us your A1C was over 10 and that we might need to look at part‑time in‑home care. I’ve been driving down to your apartment almost every Sunday for 6–7 hours to sort your pills and deal with bills—like that $1,842 ER statement from May that went to collections in September. I’ve called the hospital billing office and your Medicare supplemental plan twice trying to get a payment plan going, and I even set up a shared Google Sheet for your meds.

None of this is your “fault,” but it is why I’ve been so on edge. I’m scared we’re one emergency away from not making rent on Willow Bend.

On a lighter note: Lily drew you another picture of Bailey in a superhero cape. I’ll bring it next visit.

Love,
Melissa

Journal – 2024-10-30

I can’t stop thinking about how all of this is landing on Lily.

On October 14th, after that brutal day trying to finish the release timeline, I completely lost it when she spilled orange juice on her homework at the dining table. I yelled so loudly she burst into tears and said she didn’t want me walking her into Oak Grove anymore because I’m “too scary” when I’m mad. I spent the rest of the night in our bedroom in Apt 3B crying and googling “signs you’re an emotionally abusive parent.”

Then a few weeks later, in October, the school counselor from Oak Grove called. Apparently Lily told her teacher she was scared to go back to the apartment at 2147 Willow Bend, Apt 3B after hearing Jason and me argue again. They filed some kind of incident report and double‑checked our address. Hearing someone else say my child is afraid to come home was like being punched.

It doesn’t help that Jason and I have been in this weird trial‑separation‑but‑not phase since that huge fight at his parents’ place in Eugene. He’s been sleeping on the couch 5–6 nights a week since October, and we’re pretending everything is normal for Ron, Denise, and Mom because I can’t handle questions about who would get Lily if we split.

I keep telling myself I’m going to break these patterns from Dad and his drinking and the pounding on doors, but some nights it feels like I’m just replaying a different version for Lily.

Action items for myself: email therapist about an extra session; schedule one‑on‑one “fun time” with Lily this weekend (no chores, no phones).

Em,

Quick brain dump while Lily’s at after‑care and Jason’s still at his stand‑up.

Our relationship feels like it’s held together with duct tape right now. New Year’s Eve 2024 was the tipping point: Jason admitted he’d been DM’ing Lauren from college for about three months. I ended up locked in the bathroom at Willow Bend for over an hour while Lily slept, just sitting on the floor staring at the grout. Since then I’ve been checking his phone a couple of times a week like some cliché.

It’s layered on top of everything from earlier in the year. Remember when I had that panic episode in the parking lot at Oak Grove back in March and Jason had to leave a sprint review at Northline to come get me and Lily? He’s thrown out the “I feel like I have two kids” line more than once since. Between that and his drinking flare‑ups (and that awful fight at Ron and Denise’s in Eugene in October), I keep worrying that if we ever ended up in court over Lily, he’d use my anxiety against me.

We’re still doing the Sunday night calendar check‑ins, still tag‑teaming school events and soccer sign‑ups, but emotionally it feels like we’re mostly co‑parent roommates. We even managed a “date night” when Ron and Denise took Lily overnight in Eugene and we stayed at that little Airbnb downtown, but he was too wrapped up in a product launch to be present and I ended up crying in the bathroom there too.

Part of me wants to push for couples therapy again (maybe with Dr. Hannah Lee at Portland Family Counseling), and part of me just wants a week alone in a cabin with a stack of books.

Love,
M

Event: Sunday logistics + Salem run

Time: Sunday, 2024-08-25, 9:00 a.m. – 7:30 p.m.
Location: Start – 2147 Willow Bend Dr, Apt 3B (Beaverton) → Mom’s apt (Salem) → back home
Attendees: Melissa, Mom (Karen), maybe Lily

Notes:

Morning (9–11 a.m.)
- Quick calendar check with Jason over coffee (school events at Oak Grove, Jason’s sprint deadlines at his startup, my Northline stand‑ups).
- Print updated Google Sheet for Mom’s meds.
- Grab snack bag + Lily’s chapter books for the drive.

Drive to Salem (11–12)
- Call Dr. Patel’s office from the car re: Mom’s A1C over 10 and whether we can combine labs with her next Salem General appointment.

At Mom’s apartment (12–5)
- Sort Mom’s pills for the week; double‑check insulin doses.
- Call Salem Hospital billing about the $1,842 ER bill that went to collections in September; ask again about payment plan.
- Review CareCredit balance (that $3,200 from August rehab/supplies is stressing me out on top of our $9,000 in card debt).
- Check floors/rugs for fall risks after her July 18 parking lot fall.
- Talk gently (again) about in‑home care vs. assisted living near Beaverton ($1,000–$1,200 more per month?).

Drive home (5–6)
- Let Lily pick music; no heavy money talk with Jason in front of her.

Evening (6–7:30)
- Sheet‑pan dinner, bath for Lily, quick email to Emily with updates so I’m not carrying all of this alone.
- 10 minutes of yoga if I can keep my eyes open.

Journal – 2024-08-23 (post–Dr. Nguyen)

Yesterday’s primary care visit at Tualatin Valley Family Medicine rattled me more than I expected. Dr. Nguyen had me do the PHQ‑9 and GAD‑7 again; she said I’m in the “moderately severe” range for both anxiety and depression. She wrote a prescription for sertraline 50 mg and handed me a list of therapists who take Regence. I nodded, took the paper, and walked out. The prescription is still sitting unfilled because I’m scared of side effects and of being “on meds,” and honestly I’m paranoid about our health plan flagging me as having a mental health condition.

So instead I came home to Willow Bend, put Lily to bed, and did what I’ve been doing: 5 mg of melatonin, two (okay, three) glasses of wine to “take the edge off,” and the Calm app in my ears. Spoiler: I still woke up at 3 a.m. heart racing, thinking about Mom’s $3,200 CareCredit balance, the $9,000 in other cards, Jason hinting about lawyers after I missed Lily’s movie night at Oak Grove, and that awful bathroom incident at Northline where I hid during the $180k renewal review.

This isn’t working. I don’t want Lily’s memories of me to be wine breath and snapping over spilled juice.

To do:
- Look up at least three therapists from Nguyen’s list and email them.
- Talk honestly with Jason at our Sunday check‑in about the wine.
- Consider actually filling the sertraline script and giving it a fair try.

To: hr@northlinesolutions.com
Cc: erica.james@northlinesolutions.com

Subject: Request for temporary flexible schedule (family medical + childcare)

Hi HR team,

I’m writing to request a temporary adjustment to my schedule over the next few months due to a combination of family medical responsibilities and childcare needs.

Since my mom, Karen Carter, had her fall in the Salem General Hospital parking lot on July 18, 2024 and her endocrinologist (Dr. Patel) raised concerns about her A1C being over 10, I’ve been driving to Salem most Sundays for 6–7 hours to manage her medications and medical bills (including an ER bill for $1,842 that recently went to collections). In addition, Lily’s teacher at Oak Grove Elementary, Ms. Ramirez, suggested at our November 14, 2023 conference that we pursue an ADHD evaluation. I’ve booked a developmental pediatrics consult for Lily at Doernbecher Children’s Hospital on March 5, 2024 and anticipate follow‑up school meetings.

I’d like to propose shifting 5 hours of my work week to earlier mornings (6–8 a.m. blocks a few days a week) and one afternoon from the office to remote so I can attend Mom’s appointments and Lily’s school/medical visits without missing key stand‑ups or sprint reviews. I’ll continue to be available for our 9:00 a.m. daily stand‑up and major client calls, and I can share a more detailed availability grid if helpful.

I’m committed to maintaining my performance on the Aurora rollout and the Beaverton client accounts; these changes would help me manage things more sustainably and hopefully prevent the kind of burnout that led to my recent anxiety issues.

Thank you for considering this. I’m happy to discuss options.

Best,
Melissa Carter
Project Coordinator, Northline Solutions
